author: Muhammad Hasn, Hafiz
title: Rauzat-ul-Kubraviyah
gloss: A pamphlet in which the writer describes a well-known Muhammadan prayer called the Aurad-i-Fateh, by quoting traditions.
notes: Companion to Aurad-i-Qadiriya (p32 s4): consecutive regs 1256–1257, same press, litany literature in pairs.
marks: pencil '89' right margin; serial partially obscured

flags.issue: margin serial partially obscured; read as 5 (follows s4 on p32)

section: BILINGUAL (ARABIC AND PUNJABI IN PERSIAN CHARACTER)—POETRY.
author: Barkat Ali, Maulvi
title: Rail-nama urf Nasihat-nama
gloss: Admonitory verses in Punjabi in explanation of certain Arabic religious texts.
notes: 'The Railway Book, alias the Book of Counsel' — the railway as homiletic metaphor binding Arabic scripture to Punjabi verse; village author, Arya press, Muslim content.

author: Lakhi Singh, Hakim
title: Bahr-ul-Jawahir
gloss: A small dictionary of Arabic words with their meanings in Sindhi.
publisher: The author, Pir Bakhsh Khan, Sindh
copyright: Hakim Lakhi Singh, Pir Bakhsh Khan, Sindh; No. 161, dated the 25th October, 1910.
notes: A Sikh hakim in rural Sindh compiling and copyrighting an Arabic–Sindhi dictionary, printed at Lahore — the quarter's most compact demonstration that language markets ignore communal boundaries.

author: Muzaffar Ali, Hakim
title: Tarwiyat-ul-Mizma
gloss: A pamphlet in which the writer tries to prove that water possesses the properties of food.
notes: Yunani dietetics dispute in Arabic-with-Urdu — whether water is nutritive; scholastic medicine's disputational form intact in 1910.

author: Muhammad Abdul Aziz, Maulvi
title: Majmua-i-Khutab-i-Azizi
gloss: A book of sermons read at the time of marriage and the Juma (Friday) and 'Id prayers of the Muhammadans, with Urdu translation.
notes: Khutba anthology with Urdu translation — liturgical Arabic made followable; publisher M. Muhammad Muazzam is the 'books of Islam' author of p31 s95–98. Dual date: 1328 Hijri / August 1910. Serial 28 continues the annual Bilingual (Arabic and Urdu)—Religion sequence.

author: Juwala Singh, Master
title: Urdu men Angrezi ki Pehli Kitab
gloss: A primer in which English sentences are written in the Roman and Persian character, with Urdu translations.
notes: Section carries '(Designed for educational purposes.)'. English rendered in both Roman and Persian script by a soldier-teacher of the 35th Sikhs — almost certainly the same Jawala Singh as p21 s20 (there '34 Sikhs'): sepoy authors running a two-title language-primer business across script markets.
